lessons.title: Why do you need 4 satellites?
lessons.summary: 3 position unknowns + 1 for the receiver clock error.

steps.text: Placing a receiver in Zagreb. GPS wants to compute your position (X, Y, Z) by measuring how long the signal travels from each satellite.

Travel time × speed of light gives distance (a pseudorange). But the clock in your phone isn't as precise as the atomic clock in the satellite — so the receiver clock error is also unknown. That's 4 unknowns: X, Y, Z and the clock.
4 unknowns → you need at least 4 equations → at least 4 satellites. Look at the 'Satellites in view' table: those marked 'in use' enter the solution. This is why open sky beats an urban canyon.
The estimate (cyan) now tracks the truth (yellow) within a few metres — classic single-frequency GPS accuracy. The next lessons show what corrupts it and how it's defended.

lessons.title: GDOP and geometry
lessons.summary: Satellite layout multiplies error — spread is good, clustered is bad.

steps.text: Placing a receiver and starting the sim. It's not just HOW MANY satellites you see, but HOW they're spread across the sky.
GDOP (Geometric Dilution of Precision) is a multiplier: final error ≈ GDOP × measurement error. Watch GDOP in telemetry — below 2 is excellent, above 6 is poor.

When satellites are spread across the whole sky, the range-sphere intersections are 'sharp' → low GDOP. When they're clustered (e.g. buildings leave only a slice of sky), the intersections are shallow → high GDOP and larger error.
Opening the Multi-GNSS experiment: the elevation-mask slider shows PDOP rising as you 'cut off' low satellites. That's the same effect as an urban canyon.

lessons.title: The ionosphere and time of day
lessons.summary: A layer of charged particles slows the signal — most at midday.

steps.text: Placing a receiver. Before the signal reaches the ground it crosses the ionosphere — a layer of free electrons at 60–1000 km that slows it down.
Setting time to 02:00. At night the electron density (TEC) is low, so the delay is small — just a couple of metres at zenith.

Now 14:00. The Sun has ionized the atmosphere, TEC peaks, and the delay grows beyond 10 m at zenith (and more at low elevations). The Klobuchar model describes this with 8 parameters from the navigation message.

Opening the Ionosphere experiment: the diurnal curve peaks around 14:00, plus delay vs elevation. A dual-frequency receiver (L1/L2) cancels this error almost exactly — which is why premium receivers use two frequencies.

lessons.title: RAIM — integrity protection
lessons.summary: A naive spoof is caught by RAIM; a coordinated one is NOT.

steps.text: Placing a receiver and starting the sim. RAIM (Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring) checks whether all measurements agree — if one satellite 'lies', its residual sticks out.
Enabling a NAIVE spoof: the attacker shifts a few satellites independently. Their residuals stick out, RAIM isolates them and raises an alarm (red banner). Check the table — 'RAIM rejected'.

Now a COORDINATED spoof: the attacker fakes ALL satellites consistently, as if you were really elsewhere. The residuals agree, RAIM sees no problem — yet the solution is dragged away (red arrow on the globe). This is a fundamental limit of RAIM.

Turning the attack off. Lesson: RAIM protects against inconsistent errors (satellite faults, crude spoofing), but a sophisticated coordinated spoof (a self-consistent lie) needs other defences (antennas, inertial sensors, cryptography).

lessons.title: RTK to the centimetre
lessons.summary: Carrier phase + fixing the integer ambiguity = millimetres.

steps.text: Ordinary GPS measures the code (a ~300 m chip) and gives metre accuracy. RTK measures the carrier PHASE — a wave only ~19 cm long — so it's far more precise. Opening the RTK experiment.

Click 'Run'. Double differences (rover−base, sat−sat) cancel clocks and the atmosphere. What remains is the integer ambiguity N — how many WHOLE wavelengths fit in the path. The 'float' solution is decimetre-level until N is an integer.

Once the algorithm 'fixes' N to the correct integer ('Ambiguity fixed: YES'), error drops from decimetres to MILLIMETRES. This is the RTK used by surveyors and self-driving tractors. The baseline to the base must be short so the atmosphere stays common.

lessons.title: Spoofing vs jamming
lessons.summary: Deception (a false fix) versus denial (loss of fix).

steps.text: Placing a receiver and starting the sim. There are two ways to attack GPS — and it matters to tell them apart.

SPOOFING = deception. The attacker broadcasts fake signals so the receiver computes a WRONG position without realising it. Watch the red 'pull' arrow — where the attacker is dragging you. Dangerous because you still 'have a fix'.
JAMMING = denial. The attacker drowns the satellites in noise (red disk). The tracked-satellite count drops and the fix is LOST. Less subtle than spoofing, but it leaves you with no navigation.
Turning the attack off. Summary: spoofing gives false confidence (a wrong position that looks fine); jamming denies the service (no position at all). The defences differ for each.
